questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list. Explain each step with time complexity.
Explain the process of deleting the last node from a singly linked list. What special cases must be handled?
keyPoints: Handle single node case (set head = NULL)

questions.question: How would you search for an element in a singly linked list? Write the algorithm and analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Return position if found, -1 if not found

questions.question: Write a C function to count the number of nodes in a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Traverse from head using current pointer

questions.question: Explain why inserting at the beginning of a linked list is more efficient than inserting at the beginning of an array.
keyPoints: Linked list: O(1) - just update two pointers
Array: O(n) - must shift all existing elements

questions.question: What are the advantages of using a tail pointer along with head pointer in a singly linked list?
keyPoints: O(1) insertion at end instead of O(n)

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement insertion, deletion, and display operations on a singly linked list. Include functions for: (a) Insert at beginning (b) Insert at end (c) Delete by value (d) Display list.
keyPoints: Define Node structure with data and next
Insert at end: O(n) without tail pointer
Delete: handle head, middle, and last node cases
Handle all edge cases (empty list, single node)

questions.question: A singly linked list contains student records (roll number, name). Write functions to: (i) Add a student at the beginning (ii) Delete a student by roll number (iii) Display all students (iv) Find total count. Analyze time complexity of each operation.
keyPoints: Define structure with roll, name, next
Insert at beginning: O(1) - update head
Delete by value: O(n) - must traverse

questions.question: Compare array and singly linked list representations. Under what conditions would you choose one over the other? Explain with respect to insertion, deletion, searching, and memory usage.
keyPoints: Array: O(1) access, O(n) insert/delete at beginning
Linked list: O(1) insert/delete at beginning, O(n) access
Array: contiguous memory, linked list: scattered
Array: fixed size, linked list: dynamic
Choose array for frequent random access
Choose linked list for frequent insertions/deletions at beginning
